moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the classical 1.e4, controlling the center and opening lines for our queen and bishop. This move is flexible—it doesn't commit us to any specific system yet, which is important because we're about to play an offbeat opening that will surprise our opponent with unconventional ideas.

Black immediately challenges our center pawn and stakes a claim in the middle of the board. This is a solid, principled response that fights for space and prepares to develop with moves like Nf6. Black doesn't know yet that we're steering toward the unusual Tennison Gambit rather than a standard opening.

Here we introduce the Zukertort Opening (also known as the Tennison Gambit after the 19th-century master). Instead of the standard 2.exd5 or 2.Nc3, we develop our knight to f3, attacking Black's d5 pawn while maintaining flexibility. This move is tricky: we're not committing to taking the center immediately, and if Black takes our e4 pawn, we'll have interesting tactical chances with our well-placed knight. The idea is to play unconventionally and make Black solve problems rather than follow theoretical paths—perfect for throwing off prepared opponents.
